title: Designing a Vector Store for a Use Case
concept: Walking through requirements gathering: scale, latency, filtering needs, and update frequency.

exercises.prompt: To kick off this capstone unit, let's recap: What primary information do vector embeddings capture?
exercises.explain: In previous lessons, we learned that vector embeddings represent semantic meaning, which is the foundational data we store and query in vector databases.

cards.title: Before You Build
cards.body: Before you write a single line of code or spin up a vector database, you need to ask: 'How big is my data, how fast must it be, and how often does it change?' Picking the wrong setup early can crash your application or drain your budget.

cards.title: Defining Your Vector Store Needs
cards.body: Requirements gathering is the process of defining your application's constraints: scale (how many vectors), latency (how fast search must be), filtering (using metadata alongside vectors), and update frequency (how often vectors change).

cards.title: The Delivery Vehicle Analogy
cards.body: Think of choosing a vector database like picking a delivery vehicle. A bicycle is perfect for delivering hot pizzas locally (low latency, small scale), while a cargo ship is built for hauling tons of goods across the ocean (massive scale, slow updates).

cards.title: Music App Requirements
cards.body: A music streaming app needs to search 100 million songs (large scale) in under 50 milliseconds (low latency), and filter by the user's country (metadata filtering) while updating its library weekly.

cards.title: The 'One Size Fits All' Myth
cards.body: Many believe that one vector database fits all scenarios. In reality, a database built for lightning-fast sub-millisecond searches might struggle to handle millions of new vector updates per second.

cards.title: Latency Trade-offs
cards.body: Compare how latency requirements differ between a real-time chatbot and a background analysis tool.

left.text: Requires ultra-low latency (<100ms) for natural conversations.

right.text: Can tolerate high latency (minutes) to run complex, deep searches.

exercises.prompt: Which requirement refers to the total number of vector embeddings your database needs to store?
exercises.explain: Scale directly refers to the total volume of data, such as the total number of vector embeddings and their metadata, that the database must store.

exercises.prompt: Metadata filtering allows you to narrow down vector search results using criteria like tags, dates, or categories.
exercises.explain: Metadata filtering is crucial because users rarely want to search the entire world's data; they want to restrict search results to specific categories, dates, or users.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the definition of search response time:
exercises.sentence: The speed of a search response is measured by latency which dictates how fast users get results.
exercises.explain: Latency is the time it takes for a system to respond to a search request, which is critical for real-time user experiences.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI-generated requirement specification that lacks concrete engineering metrics:

options.text: We need to search 50,000 document chunks under 100ms, filtering results by user-id.
To optimize system performance, we will integrate vector attributes utilizing multi-faceted parameters for general database alignment.
Our catalog updates hourly, requiring a database that supports high-frequency writes without locking search reads.

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by an AI because it uses robotic, generic filler text rather than specific, context-appropriate system requirements.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a product recommendation system for a dynamic e-commerce site where prices and stock change every ten minutes. Which vector database architecture fits best?

options.text: A system optimized only for offline, batch vector search once a week
A pure index files approach with zero metadata support
A vector database with strong metadata filtering and high update throughput
A slow-write, massive-scale archive index without filtering

exercises.explain: A real-time inventory system requires constant updates (high update frequency) and immediate retrieval of items matching specific stores or prices (heavy metadata filtering), which points directly to selecting a system that handles dynamic metadata and rapid writes.
